subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 18, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Severity of Chronic Kidney Disease and Outcomes After Admissio
sentences: Story 1 is about Severity of Chronic Kidney Disease and Outcomes After Admission to the Intensive Care Unit..
This is a recent research article from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Participants included 531 090 consecutive adult (≥18 years) residents of Ontario, Canada, admitted to an ICU during the study period who had a baseline outpatient serum creatinine measurement, how should we interpret endent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults) compared with individuals without CKD, the severity of the disease among individuals with CKD was progressively associated with increa?
The study included 531 090 adults (mean [SD] age, 67 [15] years; 57% men) admitted to the ICU.

One in 4 individuals had preexisting CKD: stage 3a CKD, eGFR 45 to 59 mL/min/1.73 m2 (12% of adults); stage 3b CKD, eGFR 30 to 44 mL/min/1.73 m2 (7% of adults); stage 4 CKD, eGFR 15 to 29 mL/min/1.73 m2 (3% of adults); non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults).

However, the risk of mortality was lower for individuals receiving maintenance dialysis (odds ratio [OR], 1.92 [95% CI, 1.82-2.04]) compared with those with non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD (OR, 2.32 [95% CI, 2.14-2.52]).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Impact of Molecular Adsorbent Recirculating System Therapy on
sentences: Story 2 is about Impact of Molecular Adsorbent Recirculating System Therapy on Piperacillin Concentration in Critically Ill Patients: An Observational Cohort Study..
This is a cohort study from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
The mean relative reduction in piperacillin concentration per session was 34% (± 7.9%; p < 0.05).
Overall, 31.6% of post-session concentrations were less than 80 mg/L, and 38% of patients had at least one concentration outside the predefined target range.
Venous blood samples were collected immediately before and after each MARS session.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Patient versus surrogate decision making for life sustaining t
sentences: Story 3 is about Patient versus surrogate decision making for life sustaining treatment and terminal care intensity..
This is a recent research article from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adult ICU admissions between 2020 and 2023, how should we interpret Patient compared with surrogate decision making for life sustaining treatment and terminal care intensity?
Among 1,189,042 patients, surrogate-determined POLST (SD-POLST) was more than three times as prevalent as patient-determined POLST (PD-POLST).
Among 90-day decedents, PD-POLST was associated with significantly reduced odds of invasive terminal care (OR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.43-0.54).
Conversely, SD-POLST more than doubled the odds (OR, 2.16; 95% CI, 1.98-2.35).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Severity of Chronic Kidney Disease and Outcomes After Admission to the Intensive Care Unit.
articles.summary: Participants included 531 090 consecutive adult (≥18 years) residents of Ontario, Canada, admitted to an ICU during the study period who had a baseline outpatient serum creatinine measurement within 7 to 365 days prior to admission. Mortality (ICU, hospital, and 90-day mortality) and kidney replacement therapy (KRT) requirement in the ICU and dependence at 90 days. The study included 531 090 adults (mean [SD] age, 67 [15] years; 57% men) admitted to the ICU. One in 4 individuals had preexisting CKD: stage 3a CKD, eGFR 45 to 59 mL/min/1.73 m2 (12% of adults); stage 3b CKD, eGFR 30 to 44 mL/min/1.73 m2 (7% of adults); stage 4 CKD, eGFR 15 to 29 mL/min/1.73 m2 (3% of adults); non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults). However, the risk of mortality was lower for individuals receiving maintenance dialysis (odds ratio [OR], 1.92 [95% CI, 1.82-2.04]) compared with those with non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD (OR, 2.32 [95% CI, 2.14-2.52]).
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

pico.population: Participants included 531 090 consecutive adult (≥18 years) residents of Ontario, Canada, admitted to an ICU during the study period who had a baseline outpatient serum creatinine measurement
pico.intervention: endent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults)
pico.comparator: individuals without CKD, the severity of the disease among individuals with CKD was progressively associated with increa
pico.outcomes: Mortality (ICU, hospital, and 90-day mortality) and kidney replacement therapy (KRT) requirement in the ICU and dependence at 90 days.

keyResults: The study included 531 090 adults (mean [SD] age, 67 [15] years; 57% men) admitted to the ICU.

One in 4 individuals had preexisting CKD: stage 3a CKD, eGFR 45 to 59 mL/min/1.73 m2 (12% of adults); stage 3b CKD, eGFR 30 to 44 mL/min/1.73 m2 (7% of adults); stage 4 CKD, eGFR 15 to 29 mL/min/1.73 m2 (3% of adults); non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults).

However, the risk of mortality was lower for individuals receiving maintenance dialysis (odds ratio [OR], 1.92 [95% CI, 1.82-2.04]) compared with those with non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 CKD (OR, 2.32 [95% CI, 2.14-2.52]).

Risk for KRT initiation in the ICU increased with CKD severity relative to individuals without CKD: stage 3a (adjusted OR [AOR], 1.79 [95% CI, 1.68-1.90]), stage 3b (AOR, 3.02 [95% CI, 2.83-3.22]), stage 4 (AOR, 6.71 [95% CI, 6.23-7.22]), and non-dialysis-dependent stage 5 (AOR, 32.00 [95% CI, 29.07-35.22]).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

englishNotes.speakingPrompt: In one sentence, explain whether endent stage 5 CKD, eGFR less than 15 mL/min/1.73 m2 (1% of adults); and undergoing maintenance dialysis (2% of adults) should change ICU practice today.

articles.title: Impact of Molecular Adsorbent Recirculating System Therapy on Piperacillin Concentration in Critically Ill Patients: An Observational Cohort Study.
articles.summary: To assess the impact of Molecular Adsorbent Recirculating System (MARS) therapy on total plasma piperacillin concentrations in critically ill patients with acute liver failure or acute-on-chronic liver failure. The mean relative reduction in piperacillin concentration per session was 34% (± 7.9%; p < 0.05). Overall, 31.6% of post-session concentrations were less than 80 mg/L, and 38% of patients had at least one concentration outside the predefined target range. Retrospective, single-center observational cohort study. Consecutive adult patients treated with MARS therapy and piperacillin-tazobactam between March 2023 and June 2025.
articles.analysis: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.

pico.population: Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text.
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Venous blood samples were collected immediately before and after each MARS session. Total piperacillin concentrations were quantified using ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography. The primary endpoint was the relative reduction in plasma piperacillin concentration induced by MARS therapy. Thirty patients (121 piperacillin measurements across 77...

keyResults: The mean relative reduction in piperacillin concentration per session was 34% (± 7.9%; p < 0.05).
Overall, 31.6% of post-session concentrations were less than 80 mg/L, and 38% of patients had at least one concentration outside the predefined target range.
Venous blood samples were collected immediately before and after each MARS session.
Total piperacillin concentrations were quantified using ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography.
criticalAppraisal: Observational evidence: useful for association and prognosis, but residual confounding is likely.
Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.

articles.title: Patient versus surrogate decision making for life sustaining treatment and terminal care intensity.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: However, the impact of the signatory's identity-whether the patient or a surrogate-on clinical trajectories in the intensive care unit (ICU) remains poorly characterized. Among 1,189,042 patients, surrogate-determined POLST (SD-POLST) was more than three times as prevalent as patient-determined POLST (PD-POLST). Among 90-day decedents, PD-POLST was associated with significantly reduced odds of invasive terminal care (OR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.43-0.54). Conversely, SD-POLST more than doubled the odds (OR, 2.16; 95% CI, 1.98-2.35). Notably, even patients with proactive ADs experienced increased care intensity once a surrogate signed the final order (OR, 1.69; 95% CI, 1.51-1.89), indicating a phenomenon of "AD erosion." SD-POLST was also associated with significantly higher daily hospitalization costs (cost ratio, 1.04; 95% CI, 1.02-1.06) compared with no documentation.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

Among 90-day decedents, PD-POLST was associated with significantly reduced odds of invasive terminal care (OR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.43-0.54).
Conversely, SD-POLST more than doubled the odds (OR, 2.16; 95% CI, 1.98-2.35).

Notably, even patients with proactive ADs experienced increased care intensity once a surrogate signed the final order (OR, 1.69; 95% CI, 1.51-1.89), indicating a phenomenon of "AD erosion." SD-POLST was also associated with significantly higher daily hospitalization costs (cost ratio, 1.04; 95% CI, 1.02-1.06) compared with no documentation.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
